items.q: What is transpiration in the water cycle?
items.correct: The loss of water vapour from plant leaves to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The movement of water through soil into rivers.
items.random_wrong: The freezing of water into ice in glaciers.
items.why: Students confuse transpiration from plants with runoff or infiltration in the water cycle.

items.q: Why is most atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to plants directly?
items.correct: Nitrogen gas (N2) is very unreactive and must be fixed into compounds before plants can use it.
items.misconception: There is too little nitrogen in the air for plants to use.
items.random_wrong: Plants cannot absorb any gases at all.
items.why: Learners assume scarcity rather than the chemical inertness of N2 as the reason nitrogen needs fixing.

items.q: Does the phosphorus cycle have a significant gaseous (atmospheric) phase?
items.correct: No; phosphorus cycles mainly through rock, soil and water with no major gaseous stage.
items.misconception: Yes; phosphorus moves largely as a gas like carbon and nitrogen.
items.random_wrong: Phosphorus does not cycle at all.
items.why: Students assume all biogeochemical cycles have a gas phase, overlooking that phosphorus is a sedimentary cycle.

items.q: Which cycle has no significant atmospheric phase?
items.why: It's Phosphorus. “Carbon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Average time in a reservoir is called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Residence time. “Flux” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate residence time of CO2 in the atmosphere in years (centennial estimate)?
items.why: The real figure is 100 years. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
